I am surprised by following code.  I thought we converted all 'lists of commands' to 'one ssh session per command' but then I saw this edit below.   Help me understand....

⏺ Update(Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh)
  ⎿  Added 2 lines, removed 2 lines
      811          "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
      812          "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null" \
      813          "mkpasswd -l -u '${z_wlu}' >> /etc/passwd"                        \
      814 -        "mkdir -p '/home/${z_wlu}'"                                       \
      815 -        "chown -R '${z_wlu}' '/home/${z_wlu}'"
      814 +        "mkdir -p '${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}'"                         \

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

783	zbujb_garrison_step3_create() {
784	  local z_letter="${1:-}"
785	  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
786	  buc_step "  [3/6] Create workload (${z_wlu})"
787	
788	  case "${z_letter}" in
789	    b)
790	      case "${BURN_PLATFORM}" in
791	        bubep_linux)
792	          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
793	            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
794	            "sudo -n useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash '${z_wlu}'"  \
795	            "sudo -n passwd  --lock '${z_wlu}'"
796	          ;;
797	        bubep_mac)
798	          # Mac uses dscl/sysadminctl; left for in-environment refinement.
799	          # Operator may need to seat a more idiomatic primary group ID.
800	          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
801	            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
802	            "sudo -n sysadminctl -addUser '${z_wlu}' -roleAccount"        \
803	            "sudo -n dscl . -create '${BUJB_path_mac_user_home}' UserShell /bin/bash"
804	          ;;
805	      esac
806	      ;;
807	    c)
808	      # Cygwin reflects Windows user accounts; mint via net.exe with a
809	      # disabled-password posture (we want ssh-key-only).
810	      zbujb_admin_exec c                                                  \
811	        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
812	        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null" \
813	        "mkpasswd -l -u '${z_wlu}' >> /etc/passwd"                        \
814	        "mkdir -p '${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}'"                         \
815	        "chown -R '${z_wlu}' '${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}'"
816	      ;;
817	    w)
818	      # Windows-side workload account only. The WSL-side Linux user is
819	      # provisioned later inside the workload's *own* imported rbtww-main
820	      # distribution by zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl — admin's WSL is not
821	      # the workload's runtime distribution under the redesigned
822	      # garrison-w (Microsoft per-user WSL constraint, BUSJGW).
823	      zbujb_admin_exec w                                                  \
824	        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
825	        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null"
826	      # OpenSSH-Win32 silently closes at preauth if the workload SID has
827	      # no HKLM\...\ProfileList entry. net.exe user /add creates the SAM
828	      # entry but not the profile registration. Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383.
829	      # Bash-orchestrated per WSG-PS-5: capture SID, build registry path
830	      # in bash, then two atomic single-expression PS calls.
831	      local z_sid z_regkey
832	      z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") \
833	        || buc_die "step 3 (w): could not resolve SID for ${z_wlu}"
834	      z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
835	      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
836	      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'ProfileImagePath' -Value '${BUJB_path_winenv_user_home}' -PropertyType ExpandString -Force | Out-Null"
837	      ;;
838	  esac
839	}

}

# Step 3 -- create the workload account fresh, ssh-only, no privilege.
zbujb_garrison_step3_create() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  buc_step "  [3/6] Create workload (${z_wlu})"

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      case "${BURN_PLATFORM}" in
        bubep_linux)
          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
            "sudo -n useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash '${z_wlu}'"  \
            "sudo -n passwd  --lock '${z_wlu}'"
          ;;
        bubep_mac)
          # Mac uses dscl/sysadminctl; left for in-environment refinement.
          # Operator may need to seat a more idiomatic primary group ID.
          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
            "sudo -n sysadminctl -addUser '${z_wlu}' -roleAccount"        \
            "sudo -n dscl . -create '${BUJB_path_mac_user_home}' UserShell /bin/bash"
          ;;
      esac
      ;;
    c)
      # Cygwin reflects Windows user accounts; mint via net.exe with a
      # disabled-password posture (we want ssh-key-only).
      zbujb_admin_exec c                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null" \
        "mkpasswd -l -u '${z_wlu}' >> /etc/passwd"                        \
        "mkdir -p '${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}'"                         \
        "chown -R '${z_wlu}' '${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}'"
      ;;
    w)
      # Windows-side workload account only. The WSL-side Linux user is
      # provisioned later inside the workload's *own* imported rbtww-main
      # distribution by zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl — admin's WSL is not
      # the workload's runtime distribution under the redesigned
      # garrison-w (Microsoft per-user WSL constraint, BUSJGW).
      zbujb_admin_exec w                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null"
      # OpenSSH-Win32 silently closes at preauth if the workload SID has
      # no HKLM\...\ProfileList entry. net.exe user /add creates the SAM
      # entry but not the profile registration. Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383.
      # Bash-orchestrated per WSG-PS-5: capture SID, build registry path
      # in bash, then two atomic single-expression PS calls.
      local z_sid z_regkey
      z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") \
        || buc_die "step 3 (w): could not resolve SID for ${z_wlu}"
      z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'ProfileImagePath' -Value '${BUJB_path_winenv_user_home}' -PropertyType ExpandString -Force | Out-Null"
      ;;
  esac
}

content.content:
350	}
351	
352	# zbujb_admin_exec LETTER STMT [STMT ...] -- run statements as the privileged
353	# user on the remote node. Statements are IFS-joined with ';' and shipped
354	# as the bash -c argument; embedded " characters are escaped to \" so the
355	# outer "..." of `bash -c "..."` survives the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser
356	# layer (relevant for c/w letters via Windows OpenSSH). Linux bash inside
357	# "..." applies the same \" → " rule, so b letter gets equivalent semantics.
358	#
359	# No stdin pipe to ssh, no inner shell process reading a script source,
360	# no base64 detour, no pipeline-in-$() (BCG line 502), no temp file. The
361	# remote bash receives the script as its -c argument and executes it
362	# directly. Caller redirects stdout/stderr for capture; returns ssh exit
363	# code. Each positional statement should be a complete shell statement;
364	# the joiner inserts ';' between them so the body fits one line (cmd.exe
365	# handles multi-line args poorly).
366	#
367	# This function does NOT transform $. Body authors are responsible for
368	# per-letter $-escape discipline (see WSG).
369	zbujb_admin_exec() {
370	  zbujb_sentinel
371	  local z_letter="${1:-}"
372	  zbujb_assert_shell_letter "${z_letter}"
373	  shift
374	  test $# -ge 1 \
375	    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_exec: at least one statement required"
376	
377	  # IFS-local join with ';' — pure parameter expansion, no subprocess.
378	  local IFS=';'
379	  local z_body="$*"
380	
381	  # Escape " → \" for the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer.
382	  local z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"
383	
384	  local z_remote_invoker
385	  case "${z_letter}" in
386	    b) z_remote_invoker='bash' ;;
387	    c) z_remote_invoker='C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login' ;;
388	    w) z_remote_invoker="wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root bash" ;;
389	  esac
390	
391	  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"     \
392	      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                         \
393	      -o BatchMode=yes                              \
394	      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new           \
395	      -o ConnectTimeout=15                          \
396	      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
397	      "${z_remote_invoker} -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
398	}
399	
400	# zbujb_admin_powershell BODY... -- run a PowerShell statement chain on
401	# the remote node as the privileged user. Prepends discipline prelude:
402	# $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' (PS errors terminate), $env:WSL_UTF8=1
403	# (wsl.exe emits UTF-8), $LASTEXITCODE=0 (initialize so the trailing
404	# if-check doesn't trip on the $null default — $null -ne 0 evaluates True
405	# in PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
406	# discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like Get-LocalUser
407	# whose tables haven't flushed to stdout yet). Trailing if-check
408	# propagates $LASTEXITCODE on non-zero from native commands in the body.
409	# Caller redirects stdout/stderr; returns ssh's exit code.
410	zbujb_admin_powershell() {
411	  zbujb_sentinel
412	  test $# -ge 1 \
413	    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_powershell: PowerShell command body required"
414	  local -r z_body="$*"
415	
416	  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
417	      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
418	      -o BatchMode=yes                             \
419	      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new          \
420	      -o ConnectTimeout=15                         \
421	      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"       \

}

# zbujb_admin_exec LETTER STMT [STMT ...] -- run statements as the privileged
# user on the remote node. Statements are IFS-joined with ';' and shipped
# as the bash -c argument; embedded " characters are escaped to \" so the
# outer "..." of `bash -c "..."` survives the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser
# layer (relevant for c/w letters via Windows OpenSSH). Linux bash inside
# "..." applies the same \" → " rule, so b letter gets equivalent semantics.
#
# No stdin pipe to ssh, no inner shell process reading a script source,
# no base64 detour, no pipeline-in-$() (BCG line 502), no temp file. The
# remote bash receives the script as its -c argument and executes it
# directly. Caller redirects stdout/stderr for capture; returns ssh exit
# code. Each positional statement should be a complete shell statement;
# the joiner inserts ';' between them so the body fits one line (cmd.exe
# handles multi-line args poorly).
#
# This function does NOT transform $. Body authors are responsible for
# per-letter $-escape discipline (see WSG).
zbujb_admin_exec() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  zbujb_assert_shell_letter "${z_letter}"
  shift
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_exec: at least one statement required"

  # IFS-local join with ';' — pure parameter expansion, no subprocess.
  local IFS=';'
  local z_body="$*"

  # Escape " → \" for the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer.
  local z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"

  local z_remote_invoker
  case "${z_letter}" in
    b) z_remote_invoker='bash' ;;
    c) z_remote_invoker='C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login' ;;
    w) z_remote_invoker="wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root bash" ;;
  esac

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                         \
      -o BatchMode=yes                              \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new           \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                          \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      "${z_remote_invoker} -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
}

# zbujb_admin_powershell BODY... -- run a PowerShell statement chain on
# the remote node as the privileged user. Prepends discipline prelude:
# $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' (PS errors terminate), $env:WSL_UTF8=1
# (wsl.exe emits UTF-8), $LASTEXITCODE=0 (initialize so the trailing
# if-check doesn't trip on the $null default — $null -ne 0 evaluates True
# in PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
# discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like Get-LocalUser
# whose tables haven't flushed to stdout yet). Trailing if-check
# propagates $LASTEXITCODE on non-zero from native commands in the body.
# Caller redirects stdout/stderr; returns ssh's exit code.
zbujb_admin_powershell() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_powershell: PowerShell command body required"
  local -r z_body="$*"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                             \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new          \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                         \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"       \
      "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
}

# zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE BODY -- run a single PS expression on the
# remote node as the named role and emit its stdout. Windows CR is stripped
# via bash parameter expansion (no subprocess) so $(...) yields a clean
# value. Single-expression discipline per WSG-PS-5; multi-statement bodies
# do not belong here. Caller || buc_die on capture failure.
# ROLE: zbujb_privileged | zbujb_workload
zbujb_powershell_capture() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test $# -ge 2 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: ROLE BODY required"
  local -r z_role="$1"; shift
  local -r z_body="$*"

  local z_key z_user
  case "${z_role}" in
    zbujb_privileged) z_key="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"; z_user="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}" ;;
    zbujb_workload)   z_key="${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}";   z_user="${BUJB_workload_user}"   ;;
    *)                buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: unknown role '${z_role}' (zbujb_privileged|zbujb_workload)" ;;
  esac

  local z_out z_exit=0
  z_out=$(ssh -i "${z_key}"                          \
              -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                  \
              -o BatchMode=yes                       \
              -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new    \

lastPrompt: I am surprised by following code.  I thought we converted all 'lists of commands' to 'one ssh session per command' but then I saw this edit below.   Help me understand....  ⏺ Update(Tools/buk/bujb_jur…

aiTitle: Understand SSH session command consolidation pattern

content.text:
That edit didn't change the call shape — it changed two path values inside an already-existing 5-statement argument list. And the call shape is one ssh session, not many.

`zbujb_admin_exec` (bujb_jurisdiction.sh:369-398) takes `LETTER STMT [STMT ...]` and:

1. IFS-joins the statements with `;` (line 378-379) → single body string
2. Escapes `"` → `\"` for the cmd.exe layer (line 382)
3. Fires **one** ssh invocation (line 391-397), passing `bash -c "joined;body;here"` as the remote command

So a 5-element STMT list ≠ 5 ssh sessions. It's one ssh, one remote `bash -c`, with the statements stitched together by `;`. The function's docstring even calls this out: "No stdin pipe to ssh, no inner shell process reading a script source... The remote bash receives the script as its -c argument and executes it directly."

The conversion you're remembering ("one ssh session per command") likely refers to:
- Eliminating heredoc-fed inner shells / piped script bodies
- Single `bash -c` argument instead of multiline transport
- Per-call atomicity, not per-statement

The variadic-statements pattern is the *result* of that conversion, not a regression from it. Each `zbujb_admin_exec` call = one ssh round-trip carrying a `;`-joined one-liner.

The diff you're looking at swapped hardcoded `/home/${z_wlu}` for `${BUJB_path_posix_user_home}` in two of the five statements — purely a path-source refactor inside the existing single-session call.

content.text:
Right — `;` by itself does not propagate failure. `bash -c "stmt1;stmt2;stmt3"` runs all three even if `stmt1` fails, and the exit code is `stmt3`'s. So a failing `mkdir` followed by a successful `chown` would return 0 and look fine.

The convention that defuses this in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is the leading `set -euo pipefail` statement. Look at every multi-statement `zbujb_admin_exec` call — they all start with it (lines 793, 801, 811, 824, 896, 908, 986). With `-e`, the remote bash dies on the first non-zero exit and ssh propagates that exit code to the caller. That's what makes the `;`-stitched body safe.

Two real caveats worth noting:

1. **The convention is load-bearing and unenforced.** Nothing in `zbujb_admin_exec` itself prepends `set -e`. If a future author drops or forgets the leading statement, all errors except the last one go silent. The function's docstring should probably warn about this — currently it doesn't.

2. **Lines 762-771 deliberately use `set -uo pipefail` (no `-e`).** Those are the obliterate paths, with `|| true` on each statement to tolerate "user already gone." That's an intentional exception — destruction is opportunistic, not transactional — but it shows the convention isn't uniform.

3. **`set -e` has well-known holes** even when present: failures inside `if`/`while`/`||`/`&&` chains, command substitutions in older bash, etc. None of the current call sites trip these, but future statements could.

So your instinct is right: `;` stitching is a footgun, and the only thing keeping it honest is a one-token discipline (`set -e` first) at every call site. The alternative — separate ssh calls per statement (the pattern at lines 928-932 with `zbujb_place_trust_run`) — gets you per-statement atomicity and named failure points at the cost of N round-trips.

local z_target_dir="${z_target%/*}"  # parameter expansion replaces dirname
  buc_step "  [5/6] Plant workload privkey (${z_target})"

  openssl enc -base64 -A < "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
      > "${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDOUT}" \
      2> "${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDERR}" \
    || buc_die "base64 encode failed for workload key: ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE} — see ${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDERR}"
  local z_key_b64
  z_key_b64=$(<"${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDOUT}")
  z_key_b64="${z_key_b64//$'\n'/}"

  local z_sudo_prefix=""
  test "${z_letter}" != "b" || z_sudo_prefix="sudo -n "

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b|w)
      zbujb_admin_exec "${z_letter}"                                      \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "ztmp=\$(mktemp)"                                                 \
        "trap 'rm -f \"\\\${ztmp}\"' EXIT"                                \
        "echo '${z_key_b64}' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\\\${ztmp}\"" \
        "${z_sudo_prefix}mkdir -p   '${z_target_dir}'"                    \
        "${z_sudo_prefix}install -m 600 -o '${z_wlu}' -g '${z_wlu}' \"\\\${ztmp}\" '${z_target}'"
      ;;
    c)
      zbujb_admin_exec c                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "ztmp=\$(mktemp)"                                                 \
        "trap 'rm -f \"\${ztmp}\"' EXIT"                                  \
        "echo '${z_key_b64}' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\${ztmp}\"" \
        "mkdir -p   '${z_target_dir}'"                                    \
        "cp \"\${ztmp}\" '${z_target}'"                                   \
        "chmod 600  '${z_target}'"                                        \
        "chown      '${z_wlu}' '${z_target}'"
      ;;
  esac
}

# Each step propagates failure via zbujb_admin_powershell + || buc_die.
bujb_wsl_install() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test "${ZBUJB_RESOLVED:-}" = "1" \
    || buc_die "bujb_wsl_install: call bujb_resolve_investiture first"

  zbujb_wsl_install_assert_platform

  local -r z_seed='Ubuntu-24.04'
  local -r z_install_dir='C:\WSL'
  local -r z_distro_dir="${z_install_dir}\\${BUJB_wsl_distribution}"
  local -r z_tar_path="${z_install_dir}\\${BUJB_wsl_distribution}.tar"

  buc_step "WSL Install: ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} on ${BURN_HOST} (seed: ${z_seed})"

  buc_step "  [1/6] Purge prior state (idempotent: unregister both, remove tar+dir)"
  local -r z_purge_body="if ((wsl.exe --list --quiet) -match '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}') { wsl.exe --unregister ${BUJB_wsl_distribution}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE } }; if ((wsl.exe --list --quiet) -match '${z_seed}') { wsl.exe --unregister ${z_seed}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE } }; if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }; if (Test-Path '${z_distro_dir}') { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_distro_dir}' }"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "${z_purge_body}" \
    || buc_die "Purge step failed"

  buc_step "  [2/6] Ensure ${z_install_dir} directory"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path '${z_install_dir}' -Force | Out-Null" \
    || buc_die "Failed to create ${z_install_dir}"

  buc_step "  [3/6] Install ${z_seed} seed distribution"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --install --no-launch -d ${z_seed}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to install ${z_seed} seed"

  buc_step "  [4/6] Export ${z_seed} to ${z_tar_path}"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --export ${z_seed} '${z_tar_path}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to export ${z_seed}"

  buc_step "  [5/6] Import ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} from ${z_tar_path}"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --import ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} '${z_distro_dir}' '${z_tar_path}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to import ${BUJB_wsl_distribution}"

  buc_step "  [6/6] Cleanup: unregister ${z_seed} and remove .tar"
  local -r z_cleanup_body="wsl.exe --unregister ${z_seed}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }; if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
  zbujb_admin_powershell "${z_cleanup_body}" \
    || buc_die "Cleanup step failed"

  buc_step "WSL Install succeeded"
}

######################################################################
# Public: Privileged SSH (thin admin-side pass-through)

# bujb_privileged_ssh COMMAND... -- run an arbitrary command on the BURN
# node as BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER under key-only auth. Pass-through: argv is
# handed to ssh as the remote command without shell wrapping (operator
# prepends `powershell -Command`, `bash -c`, etc. as the platform requires).
# Caller must have invoked bujb_resolve_investiture beforehand. Returns
# ssh's exit code.
bujb_privileged_ssh() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test "${ZBUJB_RESOLVED:-}" = "1" \
    || buc_die "bujb_privileged_ssh: call bujb_resolve_investiture first"
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "bujb_privileged_ssh: command required"

  buc_step "Privileged SSH: ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST} (${BUZ_FOLIO})"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                        \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"      \
      "$@"                                        \

zbujb_fenestrate_exec_with_password_fallback() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_stdout_file="${1:-}"
  local -r z_stderr_file="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_stdout_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_exec_with_password_fallback: stdout_file required"
  test -n "${z_stderr_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_exec_with_password_fallback: stderr_file required"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                                \
      -o BatchMode=no                                      \
      -o PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password       \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new                  \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                                 \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      'powershell -NoProfile -File -'                      \
      > "${z_stdout_file}"                                 \
      2> "${z_stderr_file}"
}

# zbujb_fenestrate_exec_keyonly STDOUT_FILE STDERR_FILE
# Same as above but BatchMode=yes (no password fallback). Used for phase 2.
zbujb_fenestrate_exec_keyonly() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_stdout_file="${1:-}"
  local -r z_stderr_file="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_stdout_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_exec_keyonly: stdout_file required"
  test -n "${z_stderr_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_exec_keyonly: stderr_file required"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                                \
      -o BatchMode=yes                                     \
      -o PreferredAuthentications=publickey                \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new                  \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                                 \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      'powershell -NoProfile -File -'                      \
      > "${z_stdout_file}"                                 \
      2> "${z_stderr_file}"
}

# zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives REMOTE_FILE -- load REMOTE_FILE (raw
# sshd_config bytes from PowerShell Get-Content), strip CR, then assert
# each directive in BUJB_sshd_hardening appears with the expected value.
# Load-then-iterate (no nested while-read on stdin); pure parameter
# expansion + case (no awk/grep/tr).
zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_remote_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_remote_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives: remote_file required"
  test -f "${z_remote_file}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives: remote_file not found: ${z_remote_file}"

  local -r z_raw_bytes=$(<"${z_remote_file}")
  test -n "${z_raw_bytes}" || buc_die "zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives: empty remote sshd_config bytes: ${z_remote_file}"
  local -r z_clean_bytes="${z_raw_bytes//$'\r'/}"

  local z_directives_roll=()
  local z_pair=""
  while IFS= read -r z_pair || test -n "${z_pair}"; do
    test -n "${z_pair}" || continue
    z_directives_roll+=("${z_pair}")

content.text:
Survey complete. There are two `;`-stitching patterns in play and one structural snag that blocks naive splitting.

## Multi-statement bodies in this file

**`zbujb_admin_exec` (variadic STMT list, joined with `;` at line 378-379):**

| Lines | Purpose | # stmts | Splittable as-is? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 762-765 | obliterate workload (linux) | 3 | yes — independent ops |
| 768-771 | obliterate workload (mac) | 3 | yes |
| 792-795 | create workload (linux) | 3 | yes |
| 800-803 | create workload (mac) | 3 | yes |
| 810-815 | create workload (cygwin) | 5 | yes |
| 823-825 | create workload (windows) | 2 | yes |
| 895-901 | place trust (linux) | 6 | yes — file path is the only "shared" state |
| 907-913 | place trust (cygwin) | 6 | yes |
| **976-982** | plant workload key (b/w) | 6 | **NO — shares `${ztmp}` + EXIT trap** |
| **985-993** | plant workload key (c) | 8 | **NO — shares `${ztmp}` + EXIT trap** |

**`zbujb_admin_powershell` `;`-chained bodies:**

| Lines | Purpose | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| 1534 | wsl-install purge | 4-clause `if (...) { ... }` chain |
| 1555 | wsl-install cleanup | unregister `;` conditional Remove-Item |

**Not in scope** (already one-op-per-ssh, just look like multi-stmt):
- Line 930 — `set -o pipefail; echo … | openssl …` is one *pipeline*, must share its shell. One operation.
- Line 899/911 — same shape, single `echo … | tee …`.

## The structural snag

Lines 976-993 (`zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_workload_privkey`) genuinely need cross-statement state:

```
ztmp=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "${ztmp}"' EXIT
echo '<b64>' | openssl … > "${ztmp}"
install -m 600 -o user -g user "${ztmp}" /target
```

You can't split this into N ssh calls because `${ztmp}` (and the trap that cleans it) lives in the remote bash process. The repair is to eliminate the temp file entirely:

```
echo '<b64>' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A | install -m 600 -o user -g user /dev/stdin /target
```

That's one pipeline, no temp file, no trap. Need to verify GNU `install`, BSD `install` (mac), and cygwin `install` all accept `/dev/stdin` — if any don't, fall back to a deterministic path written-then-moved as two ops, or use `tee` with `chmod`/`chown` follow-up (which IS splittable across ssh calls because the path is stable).

The PowerShell `if`-chains (1534, 1555) split mechanically — copy the Test-Path → conditional Remove-Item pattern already used at lines 632-647 / 657-672.

## Proposed pace shape

I'd scope this as **one pace, intricate but mechanical**, with the structural snag locked as a decision up front:

**Silks**: `one-command-per-ssh-session`

**Character**: intricate but mechanical — most call sites split trivially; one needs a no-temp-file rewrite first.

**Goal**: Every `zbujb_admin_exec` and `zbujb_admin_powershell` invocation carries exactly one logical operation; the variadic STMT contract on `zbujb_admin_exec` is removed.

**Done looks like**:
- `zbujb_admin_exec` signature is `LETTER STMT` (single, not variadic); function rejects extra args
- `grep -nE 'zbujb_admin_(exec|powershell)' bujb_jurisdiction.sh` shows no body containing `;` outside a single-pipeline context
- `set -euo pipefail` no longer appears as a leading prefix statement (no longer needed when bodies are single ops)
- Existing exit-on-failure semantics preserved at every call site via `|| buc_die "step name failed"`

**Locked decision** (slate-time): the plant-key path uses `openssl … | install /dev/stdin /target` if all three platforms accept `/dev/stdin`; otherwise the cygwin/mac branch falls back to `tee + chmod + chown` (three ssh calls operating on a stable path). Mount agent verifies platform support before proceeding.

**Scope NOT in this pace**: the `bujb_fenestrate_*` PS-via-stdin path (different hazard category — script body, not stitching); the `bujb_wsl_install` body construction style (covered by the same conversion but lives in a separate function — could fold in or split into a follow-up).

Two questions before I'd slate this:

questions.question: Should bujb_wsl_install's two `;`-chained PS bodies (lines 1534, 1555) be folded into the same pace, or split into a follow-up?

options.label: Fold into same pace
options.description: One pace covers all multi-statement bodies in the file — admin_exec calls AND the two PS bodies. Larger pace, full sweep.

options.label: Follow-up pace
options.description: First pace covers admin_exec call sites only. wsl-install conversion gets its own pace — keeps each pace's blast radius smaller.

questions.question: Should `zbujb_admin_exec`'s contract change to refuse multi-statement bodies, or keep variadic and just stop using it that way?

options.label: Tighten contract (recommended)
options.description: Function rejects more than one STMT. Compiler-style enforcement — no future regression possible. Updates the docstring to match.

options.label: Keep variadic, change discipline only
options.description: Function still accepts STMT list; we just stop calling it that way. Lower blast radius (no function-signature change), but the footgun stays loaded.

content.text:
Good — both broader-scope choices, which makes the pace cleaner (one full sweep, contract enforced).

Here's the proposed docket:

```
## Character
Intricate but mechanical — most call sites split trivially;
one call site (plant workload key) needs a no-temp-file rewrite first.

## Goal
Every `zbujb_admin_exec` and `zbujb_admin_powershell` invocation in
`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` carries exactly one logical operation
(a single command or single pipeline). The variadic STMT list contract
on `zbujb_admin_exec` is removed.

## Locked decisions
- `zbujb_admin_exec` signature becomes `LETTER STMT` (single, not
  variadic); function dies on extra args. Docstring updated.
- The plant-key path (currently shares `${ztmp}` + EXIT trap across
  statements) is rewritten to one pipeline:
  `openssl enc -base64 -d -A | install -m 600 -o user -g user /dev/stdin /target`
  if linux GNU install, mac BSD install, AND cygwin install all accept
  `/dev/stdin`. Mount agent verifies per-platform before proceeding;
  any rejecter falls back to `tee + chmod + chown` on a stable path
  (three ssh calls).
- `bujb_wsl_install`'s two `;`-chained PowerShell bodies split using
  the Test-Path → conditional Remove-Item pattern already established
  in `zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces`.

## Done looks like
- No body of `zbujb_admin_exec` or `zbujb_admin_powershell` contains
  `;` outside a single-pipeline context.
- No `set -euo pipefail` leading-prefix statement remains in admin_exec
  calls (no longer needed at one-op-per-ssh granularity).
- Each split site preserves exit-on-failure via `|| buc_die "<step>"`.
- Garrison + fenestrate + wsl-install workflows still pass end-to-end
  on a fresh BURN node (operator-verified — no automated coverage).

## Discovery
- Multi-statement admin_exec sites: continuation-line statements,

# Step 5 -- copy workload privkey to the remote at the shell-letter's
# hardcoded destination path, with workload ownership and 0600 mode.
zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_key() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step5: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
  local z_target="${z_wlhome}/${BUJB_workload_keypath}"
  local z_target_dir="${z_target%/*}"  # parameter expansion replaces dirname
  buc_step "  [5/6] Plant workload privkey (${z_target})"

  openssl enc -base64 -A < "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
      > "${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDOUT}" \
      2> "${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDERR}" \
    || buc_die "base64 encode failed for workload key: ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE} — see ${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDERR}"
  local z_key_b64
  z_key_b64=$(<"${ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDOUT}")
  z_key_b64="${z_key_b64//$'\n'/}"

  local z_sudo_prefix=""
  test "${z_letter}" != "b" || z_sudo_prefix="sudo -n "

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b|w)
      zbujb_admin_exec "${z_letter}"                                      \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "ztmp=\$(mktemp)"                                                 \
        "trap 'rm -f \"\\\${ztmp}\"' EXIT"                                \
        "echo '${z_key_b64}' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\\\${ztmp}\"" \
        "${z_sudo_prefix}mkdir -p   '${z_target_dir}'"                    \
        "${z_sudo_prefix}install -m 600 -o '${z_wlu}' -g '${z_wlu}' \"\\\${ztmp}\" '${z_target}'"
      ;;
    c)
      zbujb_admin_exec c                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "ztmp=\$(mktemp)"                                                 \
        "trap 'rm -f \"\${ztmp}\"' EXIT"                                  \
        "echo '${z_key_b64}' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\${ztmp}\"" \
        "mkdir -p   '${z_target_dir}'"                                    \
        "cp \"\${ztmp}\" '${z_target}'"                                   \
        "chmod 600  '${z_target}'"                                        \
        "chown      '${z_wlu}' '${z_target}'"
      ;;
  esac
}

# zbujb_garrison_w_preflight -- pre-flight for shell-letter w only.
# Asserts BUJB_wsl_distribution is installed on the Windows host before any
# WSL-targeted action runs; replaces opaque downstream wsl.exe failures with
# a copy-paste-ready operator hint pointing at the privileged-SSH tabtarget.

content.text:
Yes — the snag is in `zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_key` (lines 951-994). Here's the circumstance reconstructed:

## What it does

Plants the workload private key on the remote at `<workload-home>/<keypath>` with mode 0600 owned by the workload user. Because it's a private key, the disposition has to never leave the plaintext on disk with wrong perms or wrong owner.

## How it does it today

1. Admin-side: base64-encode the key and embed the b64 string in the command body (transport via argv, no temp file on the curia side beyond the b64 sink files).
2. Remote-side, all in one bash session:
   ```
   ztmp=$(mktemp)                      # privileged user's temp, mode 0600
   trap 'rm -f "${ztmp}"' EXIT         # cleanup on shell exit
   echo '<b64>' | openssl -d > "${ztmp}"
   mkdir -p '<target_dir>'
   install -m 600 -o user -g user "${ztmp}" '<target>'   # b/w
   ```
   The cygwin branch is worse — `cp + chmod + chown` instead of `install`, so plaintext sits at `<target>` with default umask perms in the window before `chmod` fires.

## Why the temp file exists

- `openssl -d` needs an output sink before perms/owner are set
- `install -m -o -g` does write + chmod + chown atomically — the temp is the input to that atomic install
- The trap is the cleanup belt for the temp

The ztmp variable + the trap + the install all need to share a shell. That's why this body is multi-statement; it's not gratuitous, it's how the atomicity is achieved.

## Why splitting naively breaks

If we split into N ssh calls, `${ztmp}` evaporates with the first session, the trap fires on session close (deleting the file), and the second session has nothing to install from. Even if we used a stable path (not mktemp), the perms window between "decoded file lands" and "chmod fires" is real and exploitable on a multi-tenant box.

## Disposition options

**A. Single-pipeline `/dev/stdin` (no temp file at all)**
```
set -o pipefail
echo '<b64>' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A | install -D -m 600 -o user -g user /dev/stdin '<target>'
```
One pipeline = one operation. `install -D` makes `mkdir -p` redundant. No temp, no trap, no cross-statement state. But: BSD `install` on macOS is the unknown — GNU coreutils (linux b, cygwin c, wsl w) handle `/dev/stdin` cleanly; mac BSD `install` may or may not. Needs a one-shot probe.

**B. Stable path + umask discipline (two ssh calls)**
```
ssh #1: mkdir -p '<target_dir>'
ssh #2: set -o pipefail
        (umask 077; echo '<b64>' | openssl -d > '<target>') &&
        chown user:user '<target>'
```
The `umask 077` in a subshell forces 0600 from creation — no perms window. Owner is fixed in the same pipeline via `&&`. Still technically one statement (compound `(...)` && chown is one logical operation), but feels more like two. Works on every platform.

**C. scp the key directly, then `install` on remote**
```
scp the workload key to a stable path on the remote (scp default mode 0600)
ssh: install -m 600 -o user -g user '<scp_path>' '<target>'
ssh: rm '<scp_path>'
```
Avoids embedding key material in argv (which is currently visible briefly to anyone running `ps` on the remote). But scp gets ugly for the `w` letter — Windows OpenSSH serves Windows paths, not WSL paths, so we'd scp to Windows side then bridge into WSL with another command. Scope creep.

## My recommendation

**Option A as primary with mount-time mac probe**, falling back to **Option B for mac only** if BSD install rejects `/dev/stdin`.

This keeps the docket's "one operation per ssh" contract intact (a pipeline IS one operation — line 930 already establishes this precedent), eliminates the temp file and the trap entirely on the b/w/c paths that have GNU install, and contains the mac quirk to a single branch.

The docket's "no `;` outside a single-pipeline context" should be tightened to read **"outside a single-pipeline context, where `set -o pipefail` may precede the pipeline"** — that's the line-930 precedent and it's load-bearing here.

Want me to revise the docket to lock this disposition in?

message.content: I really don't like this use of temp files.  'scp' looks really clean and safe: reconsider scp

content.text:
Reconsidering scp seriously. You're right that I dismissed it too quickly.

## What scp actually buys us

The current pattern's worst hazard isn't even the temp file — it's that **the base64-encoded private key sits in argv of the remote bash process** (line 978: `echo '${z_key_b64}' | openssl ...` — that whole string is the `bash -c` argument). Anyone running `ps auxww` on the remote during execution sees the entire encoded key. scp eliminates this completely — the key flows over the encrypted channel as file content, never appearing in any process's argv.

Plus: scp lands a file with mode 0600 (when invoked with `-p`, since the source key is 0600 and ssh requires it), and there's no `mktemp + trap` to manage.

## Concrete shape per letter

A deterministic staging path in the privileged user's own directory, then `install` for the atomic mode/owner change to the workload user, then explicit cleanup. Three ssh-protocol operations per letter, each one logical operation:

**Letter `b` (linux + mac, requires sudo for cross-user ownership):**
```
op 1 (scp):  scp -p '<workload_key>' priv@host:'~/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
op 2 (ssh):  sudo -n install -D -m 600 -o '<wlu>' -g '<wlu>'
                              '~/.bujb-staging-workload-key' '<target>'
op 3 (ssh):  rm -f '~/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
```

**Letter `c` (cygwin, no sudo — privileged is Windows admin, chown works via ACL):**
```
op 1 (scp):  scp -p '<workload_key>' priv@host:'~/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
op 2 (ssh):  install -D -m 600 -o '<wlu>'
                       '/home/priv/.bujb-staging-workload-key' '<target>'
op 3 (ssh):  rm -f '/home/priv/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
```

**Letter `w` (WSL, scp lands on Windows side, /mnt/c bridges into WSL):**
```
op 1 (scp):  scp -p '<workload_key>'
                 priv@host:'C:/Users/priv/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
op 2 (ssh):  wsl.exe --user root install -D -m 600 -o '<wlu>' -g '<wlu>'
                              '/mnt/c/Users/priv/.bujb-staging-workload-key'
                              '<wsl-target>'
op 3 (ssh):  powershell -Command "Remove-Item -Force
             'C:\Users\priv\.bujb-staging-workload-key'"
```

`install -D` creates the target directory atomically with the copy + mode + owner change — no separate `mkdir` step. The staging path is **deterministic** (not mktemp-randomized), so any leftover from a crashed prior run gets naturally overwritten by the next scp; cleanup is best-effort, not load-bearing.

## What this leaves on disk, briefly

I want to be honest: scp does land the key on the remote disk for a window — between op 1 completing and op 3 running (likely sub-second on a fast link, possibly several seconds on a slow one). The file is mode 0600 owned by the privileged user, in the privileged user's own directory. It's structurally the same hazard as today's `mktemp` file (privileged-owned, 0600), but the window is longer in wall-clock terms because it spans ssh round-trips instead of one bash session.

If you want to eliminate **even this brief on-disk presence**, there's a tighter variant:

## Variant: ssh-stdin, no disk landing at all

```
ssh priv@host 'sudo -n install -m 600 -o <wlu> -g <wlu> /dev/stdin <target>' \
  < '<workload_key>'
```

One operation, one ssh call, key flows over encrypted stdin into `install`'s `/dev/stdin`, lands directly at the target with correct perms+owner — never touches a staging file on the remote. GNU `install` (linux b, cygwin c, WSL w) handles `/dev/stdin` cleanly. Mac BSD `install` is the unknown.

This is structurally even cleaner — no staging path, no cleanup step, no mkdir-via-`-D` consideration — but it requires:
- `install /dev/stdin` support (verify mac BSD install)
- For letter w, ssh stdin must flow through `wsl.exe` cleanly (this is generally fine, but is a code path worth probing once before committing)

## Two questions

questions.question: Which transport should the pace lock in for plant-key?

options.label: scp + install + rm (3 ops)
options.description: Your stated preference. Brief on-disk staging in privileged dir, mode 0600. Predictable, well-trodden ssh idiom. Three operations per letter.

options.label: ssh-stdin + install /dev/stdin (1 op)
options.description: No disk landing on remote at all. One ssh call per letter. Requires verifying BSD install on mac and stdin-through-wsl.exe on letter w.

options.label: scp primary, stdin if probes pass
options.description: Slate scp as the default; mount agent probes mac BSD install + WSL stdin during work, switches to stdin variant if both pass. Belt-and-suspenders.

questions.question: Where should the staging path live (scp variant)?

options.label: Privileged user's home dotfile (recommended)
options.description: e.g., `~priv/.bujb-staging-workload-key`. Always-existing dir, no mkdir needed, deterministic name so crashed-run remnants get overwritten.

options.label: /tmp with deterministic name
options.description: e.g., `/tmp/bujb-staging-workload-key`. /tmp always exists across all platforms but is world-readable directory (file contents still 0600-protected). Cleaner if you object to dotfiles in privileged home.

content.text:
You caught me. `BURD_TEMP_DIR` is the BUK convention for ephemeral intermediate files (`bud_dispatch.sh:102`, README.md:554 — "Ephemeral temp directory, unique per invocation; safe for intermediate files"). And `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` already uses it correctly throughout the curia side — every `ZBUJB_*_STDOUT/STDERR` constant (lines 145-179) is rooted at `${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/`. I was about to propose ad-hoc dotfile paths in privileged's home, which would have ignored the convention and made the file harder to clean up.

But here's the thing — `BURD_TEMP_DIR` is **curia-side only**. It's a directory on the local machine that runs the tabtarget; it has no meaning on the remote BURN node. My staging-path question was about where to land a file on the *remote*, where there's no equivalent constant (and where you've correctly chosen not to land any file at all).

With your transport pick (**ssh-stdin + install /dev/stdin**), the question dissolves entirely:

```
ssh priv@host '<remote-shell> -c "<sudo?> install -m 600 -o <wlu> -g <wlu> \
                                  /dev/stdin <target>"'  < <workload_key>
```

- **No remote staging file** — the key flows over ssh stdin, into `install`'s `/dev/stdin`, lands at `<target>` atomically with mode and owner correct from creation.
- **No curia staging file** — the source is the workload key file itself (`BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`), which already exists and is read directly via `<` redirection. No `openssl -base64 -A` step on the curia, no `BURD_TEMP_DIR` writes for this code path. (Lines 173-174 — the `ZBUJB_KEY_B64_STDOUT/STDERR` constants and the encode step at lines 961-967 — get deleted entirely.)
- **No argv exposure** — key never appears in any `bash -c` argument string on either side.
- **One ssh call per letter** — fits the pace's "one operation per ssh" contract cleanly.

So both convention concerns get answered by the design, not by adding a constant: BUK's `BURD_TEMP_DIR` keeps its current meaning (curia-side ephemera) and isn't fought; the remote gets no staging artifact at all.

## Updated locked-decision text for the docket

> The plant-key path is rewritten to a single ssh-stdin pipeline per letter:
> `ssh ... '<remote-shell> -c "<sudo?> install -m 600 -o <wlu> -g <wlu> /dev/stdin <target>"' < ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}`
> No remote staging file. No curia base64 encode step (the `ZBUJB_KEY_B64_*` constants and the `openssl enc -base64 -A` invocation at lines 961-967 are removed). Mount agent verifies BSD `install` on macOS accepts `/dev/stdin`, and that ssh stdin flows cleanly through `wsl.exe --user root install` for letter `w`. If either probe fails, that branch falls back to a deterministic transport — but the failure mode and substitute should be re-discussed with the operator, not improvised.

Want me to revise the full docket text with this and re-show it before slating?

Ready to enroll into `A-` at first position when you give the word.

After the contract change, these either become one-op-per-call exhibits or get re-cast as "before the one-op-per-ssh discipline" historical context. input.content:
# jjezs_slate one-command-per-ssh-session

## Character
Intricate but mechanical — most call sites split trivially; plant-key needs a no-temp-file rewrite first.

## Goal
Every `zbujb_admin_exec` and `zbujb_admin_powershell` invocation in `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` carries exactly one logical operation (a single command, or a single pipeline optionally preceded by `set -o pipefail`). The variadic STMT-list contract on `zbujb_admin_exec` is removed.

## Locked decisions
- `zbujb_admin_exec` signature becomes `LETTER STMT` (single, not variadic); dies on extra args; docstring updated.
- Plant-key (`zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_key`) is rewritten to a single ssh-stdin pipeline: `install -m 600 -o <wlu> -g <wlu> /dev/stdin <target>` reading the workload key from `${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}` over ssh stdin. No remote staging file. The curia-side `openssl enc -base64 -A` encode step and the `ZBUJB_KEY_B64_*` constants are removed. Mount agent probes (a) BSD `install` on macOS accepts `/dev/stdin`, (b) ssh stdin flows cleanly through `wsl.exe --user root install` for letter `w`. Probe failure → re-discuss with operator, do not improvise a substitute.
- `bujb_wsl_install`'s two `;`-chained PS bodies (purge, cleanup) split using the Test-Path → conditional Remove-Item pattern already established in `zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces`.

## Done
- No `zbujb_admin_exec`/`_powershell` body contains `;` outside a single-pipeline context (leading `set -o pipefail` permitted).
- No `set -euo pipefail` prefix statement remains in admin_exec calls.
- Each split site preserves exit-on-failure via `|| buc_die "<step>"`.
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md` examples and contract description match the new single-statement shape (no `;`-stitched bodies depicted as canonical).
- Garrison + fenestrate + wsl-install workflows still pass end-to-end on a fresh BURN node (operator-verified).

## Discovery
Multi-statement admin_exec sites: continuation-line statements found via `grep -nE '^\s+"[^"]+"\s*\\$' Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. PS `;`-chains: `grep -n 'zbujb_admin_powershell.*;' <same file>`. Splitting precedent: `zbujb_place_trust_run` callers (one stmt per call, exit-on-failure via the wrapper).
