content.content:
1	# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
6	bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
7	layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
8	stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
9	OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.
10	
11	WSG is empirically derived. Every established rule has a verification probe
12	that demonstrates the failure mode the rule prevents. Open questions are
13	flagged explicitly and resolved by separate experiment paces, not by
14	conjecture.
15	
16	## Core Philosophy
17	
18	**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in curia
19	bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser, another
20	in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The transport
21	**transforms** the body at each boundary. Errors at any layer can be
22	silently swallowed if not explicitly trapped.
23	
24	**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** The user's command is wrapped
25	in `cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
26	rules — `"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling —
27	bite even bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.
28	
29	**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
30	output (Write-Host, Write-Output of strings) eagerly; some cmdlets'
31	formatter cycles flush only on full pipeline completion, so calling
32	`exit` mid-body (or trailer-side) discards their unformatted output.
33	The set of lazy cmdlets is not universal — `Get-LocalUser` is proven
34	lazy, `Get-Item` is proven eager. Treat the lazy default as the safe
35	assumption (PS-2). String emission always survives.
36	
37	**Trap, don't trust.** Each rule below assumes that any error which can
38	plausibly be silenced WILL be silenced if not explicitly trapped. The bias
39	is toward defensive instrumentation: surface every non-zero exit, every
40	empty stdout, every unflushed buffer, with a forensic temp file naming the
41	boundary that swallowed it.
42	
43	**Rules enumerate; they don't illustrate.** Each rule below names a closed
44	set of allowed shapes. Anything not enumerated is forbidden, even if it
45	*looks* morally similar to an allowed form. Do not reason "this is *like*
46	one expression because it's *like* one effect" — that path admits a fourth
47	shape the rule never sanctioned, and the rationalization propagates to
48	every call site that copies the precedent. When in doubt, decompose into
49	more round-trips: the cost of an extra ssh hop is small; the cost of a
50	loophole is structural.
51	
52	## Transport Stack
53	
54	A typical chain for `zbujb_admin_powershell` or `zbujb_admin_exec`:
55	
56	```
57	1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
58	2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
59	3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
60	4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
61	5. Child shell (one of):
62	   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
63	   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
64	   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
65	6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
66	```
67	
68	Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.
69	
70	## Established Rules
71	
72	### ❌ PS-1: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init
73	
74	In a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run,
75	`$LASTEXITCODE` is `$null`. PowerShell's typed comparison treats `$null -ne 0`
76	as `True`. A trailer like `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }`
77	always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (which exits 0 in
78	practice). The `exit` short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter
79	pipeline, discarding any cmdlet output buffered by Out-Default that hasn't
80	flushed to stdout yet.
81	
82	```powershell
83	# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
84	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
85	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
86	
87	# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
88	powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
89	                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
90	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
91	```
92	
93	Probe pair (verification):
94	
95	```
96	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
97	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
98	# stdout → CRLF only
99	
100	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
101	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$LASTEXITCODE = 0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
102	# stdout → full LocalUser table
103	```
104	
105	### ❌ PS-2: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush
106	
107	PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but *some* cmdlet object outputs
108	lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render). `exit` aborts the formatter
109	pipeline. Lazy outputs that haven't flushed are discarded. The cmdlets
110	that exhibit this lazy behavior are formatter-cycle-specific: `Get-LocalUser`
111	from `Microsoft.PowerShell.LocalAccounts` is proven lazy (empty stdout
112	when followed by `exit 0`); `Get-Item` from the FileSystem provider is
113	proven eager (table survives `exit 0`). Treat the rule as the safe
114	default: assume any cmdlet output is lazy unless you've checked. See
115	the OQ-6 section of `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
116	for the empirical baseline.
117	
118	```powershell
119	# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
120	powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"
121	
122	# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
123	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
124	```
125	
126	If a wrapper provides `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` initialization (PS-1), this rule
127	is moot for happy-path bodies. Failure paths still need string-materialization
128	guards if any cmdlet output must survive the trailer's exit branch.
129	
130	### ❌ SH-1: Script via stdin to bash (heredoc form / `bash -s`)
131	
132	When ssh feeds a script to remote bash via stdin — `bash -s` on the remote
133	with heredoc on the local side, or `echo BODY | bash` chains on the remote —
134	bash reads commands from FD 0. Child processes spawned by that bash inherit
135	FD 0, connected to the script-providing pipe. A child that reads FD 0
136	(notably Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop) consumes script bytes
137	bash hadn't yet parsed. Bash hits EOF prematurely, exits 0 without errors,
138	and remaining commands silently never execute.
139	
140	This is BCG's stdin-consumption discipline (BCG line 1388) at a different
141	scope.
142	
143	```bash
144	# ❌ Script via heredoc/stdin; net.exe (or any FD-0 reader) eats bytes
145	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -s" <<SCRIPT
146	set -euo pipefail
147	net.exe user 'foo' /add ... > /dev/null
148	useradd 'foo'                       # may silently never run
149	SCRIPT
150	
151	# ✅ Body as bash -c argument; bash reads body from -c, not FD 0
152	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -euo pipefail; net.exe ...; useradd 'foo'\""
153	```
154	
155	Verification: cycle-1 vs cycle-3 of correct-wsl-user-model (`₢A-AAv`).
156	Heredoc form silently dropped useradd; args form propagated correctly,
157	proven by `getent passwd bujuw_user` returning the entry post-step-3.
158	
159	### ❌ SH-2: Multi-line bodies through cmd.exe
160	
161	Cmd.exe is line-oriented. Newlines in `"..."` quoted args are unreliable
162	across the cmd.exe → child-binary boundary. Use `;` as the bash command
163	separator and ship the body as one line.
164	
165	```bash
166	# ❌ Newlines in body; cmd.exe may fragment
167	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"
168	set -e
169	useradd foo
170	\""
171	
172	# ✅ Single-line body with ; separators
173	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -e;useradd foo\""
174	```
175	
176	### ✅ SH-3: Outer `"..."` with `\"` for embedded double-quotes
177	
178	The Windows argv parser (used by wsl.exe and most Windows binaries) honors
179	`\"` inside `"..."` as an escape for literal `"`. This survives cmd.exe and
180	reaches Linux bash as `"`. Cmd.exe itself does NOT process `\"`; it passes
181	the bytes to the child whose argv parser handles the escape.
182	
183	```bash
184	# ✅ Embedded " in body
185	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"trap 'rm -f \\\"\$path\\\"' EXIT\""
186	```
187	
188	Probe: `bash -c "echo SAW: \"hello\""` → stdout `SAW: hello` (quotes
189	stripped by bash's own parsing of the unescaped `"`).
190	
191	### ✅ PS-3: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting
192	
193	Inside the outer `"..."` of `powershell -Command "..."`, single-quoted
194	strings (`'Stop'`, `'username'`) pass through cleanly. Cmd.exe does not
195	honor single quotes for arg-grouping but treats them as literal characters;
196	PowerShell parses its body and recognizes `'...'` as PS string literals.
197	
198	```bash
199	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
200	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user'\""
201	```
202	
203	This is why `zbujb_admin_powershell` consistently uses single quotes for PS
204	string literals — they nest reliably through every layer.
205	
206	### ✅ PS-4: PS-2's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
207	
208	The PS-2 lazy-flush is a property of the PowerShell body, not of how
209	PowerShell was launched. The same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits
210	empty stdout via direct cmd.exe→powershell, cygwin→powershell.exe, and
211	wsl.exe→bash→powershell.exe. The fix (Out-String materialization, or
212	PS-1's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer doesn't fire) is also
213	transport-agnostic. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
214	§OQ-6.
215	
216	### ❌ SH-4: cmd.exe does NOT process `$()` or `$var`
217	
218	Verified by direct probe: `cmd.exe /c echo "SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` emits the
219	literal string `"SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` with no expansion. Cmd.exe's
220	variable syntax is `%var%` and it has no command substitution. Therefore
221	the outer cmd.exe layer is NOT the layer that processes shell variable
222	expansion or command substitution in transit.
223	
224	This rule is structural; the corollary is OQ-1 (something else does eat `$`
225	in some chains).
226	
227	### ❌ SH-5: BCG bans extend across the transport boundary
228	
229	The bash code we author at the curia AND the bash code we author for remote
230	execution are both subject to BCG. In particular:
231	
232	- **No heredocs anywhere.** The transport must not use `bash -s` with
233	  heredoc input (SH-1 covers the failure mode); module callers must not
234	  build remote bodies via heredoc.
235	- **No pipelines inside `$()`.** This applies to module code (e.g. the
236	  ill-fated `z_b64=$(printf ... | base64 | tr -d ...)` transport) and to
237	  bodies authored for remote execution.
238	- **No unguarded `$()`.** Remote bodies that use command substitution must
239	  guard against silent failure the same way curia code does.
240	
241	The `mktemp` exception in BCG line 1473 (introspection allowlist) carries
242	through; remote bodies may use `$(mktemp)` for temp file creation. Top-level
243	pipelines (not inside `$()`) are not banned by BCG and are permitted in
244	remote bodies (see e.g. step-5's `openssl enc -base64 -d`).
245	
246	### ❌ SH-6: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv
247	
248	The wsl.exe argv-to-Linux-invocation processor performs `$name` and
249	`${name}` substitution against its own (Linux-side, root-shell startup)
250	environment before bash receives the body. Names present in that
251	environment substitute to their values; undefined names substitute to
252	empty. **Cygwin bash does not exhibit this behavior. cmd.exe alone (per
253	SH-4) also does not.** `$(...)` command substitution is unaffected (the
254	`(` after `$` does not match the `$name`/`${name}` token shape).
255	
256	Per-letter escape table:
257	
258	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
259	|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
260	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
261	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
262	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |
263	
264	Probe pair (verification, w-letter):
265	
266	```
267	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
268	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
269	# stdout: (empty)
270	
271	# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
272	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
273	# stdout: HELLO
274	```
275	
276	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-1, §OQ-2,
277	§OQ-4.
278	
279	### ✅ SH-7: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports
280	
281	Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through every tested transport:
282	cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe → bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and native
283	Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop. `set -e` aborts the script
284	on first failure and the failing exit reaches the curia unchanged.
285	Bodies that need to fail-loud should use `set -e` or explicit `exit N`.
286	
287	Probe (w-letter, set -e + false):
288	
289	```
290	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "set -e; false; echo UNREACHED"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
291	# (no UNREACHED in stdout)
292	# EXIT=1
293	```
294	
295	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-5.
296	
297	### ✅ SH-8: Multi-line bodies via file feed (when SH-2 doesn't fit)
298	
299	When a body materially exceeds what `;`-join (SH-2) can express, the
300	canonical pattern is two-phase: phase 1 ships the body as a remote file
301	via PowerShell `Set-Content` or `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText`; phase 2
302	runs `wsl.exe ... bash /path/to/file` (or `cygwin bash --login -c "bash
303	/path/to/file"`). Side benefit: bypasses wsl.exe's argv `$`-substitution
304	(SH-6) since the script body comes from disk, not argv.
305	
306	Caveats:
307	- PS `Set-Content` writes CRLF. Either use an LF-explicit writer:
308	
309	  ```powershell
310	  [System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, ($lines -join "`n"))
311	  ```
312	
313	  or normalize CRLF→LF into a *second* file before exec.
314	- Do NOT pipe the file into `bash` via stdin; that reintroduces SH-1's
315	  stdin-consumption hazard. Use `bash /path/to/file`, not `bash <file`.
316	- The two-phase approach is not atomic; the caller flow must clean up
317	  the remote temp file.
318	
319	Default discipline remains SH-2; SH-8 applies only when bodies don't fit
320	one line. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
321	§OQ-7.
322	
323	### ❌ SH-9: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport
324	
325	When a command line is routed `bash (curia) → ssh → Windows OpenSSH →
326	cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with no PowerShell or remote bash
327	layer to interpret quotes, single quotes around args are passed
328	through to the binary as part of its argv. This is the negative form
329	of PS-3.
330	
331	```bash
332	# ❌ Single quotes survive cmd.exe and become part of wsl.exe's argv.
333	# wsl.exe sees the path as `'C:\path'` (literal quotes) and fails.
334	ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs' '...' --version 2"
335	
336	# ✅ Double quotes via `\"...\"`. After bash escape they reach the wire
337	# as `"..."`; cmd.exe strips them per its native argv parser; wsl.exe
338	# receives clean paths.
339	ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
340	```
341	
342	The asymmetry: PS-3 (single quotes survive nesting) applies when there
343	is a PowerShell layer that recognizes `'...'` as a string-literal
344	delimiter. SH-9 applies when there is no such layer — only cmd.exe
345	between the wire and the binary. Single quotes are not a
346	transport-universal "safe" choice; they require a layer that
347	interprets them.
348	
349	Decision rule: pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter** in
350	the transport stack:
351	
352	| Innermost interpreter | Quote form for embedded args |
353	|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
354	| PowerShell            | `'...'` (PS string literal)   |
355	| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)  |
356	| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips) |
357	
358	Empirical site: `zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl` in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
359	— the `wsl --import` invocation in garrison-w's three-namespace
360	redesign (BUSJGW).
361	
362	### ❌ PS-5: PowerShell bodies are single expressions
363	
364	A `zbujb_admin_powershell` (or `zbujb_powershell_capture`) body MUST be
365	exactly one of these three shapes — no others:
366	
367	1. One cmdlet call (with arguments, parameters, and pipelined formatters
368	   like `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
369	2. One native binary call (with arguments).
370	3. One native binary with an inner-shell body (e.g.
371	   `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).
372	
373	The enumeration is constructive (Core Philosophy, *Rules enumerate; they
374	don't illustrate*). No other PowerShell construct may appear at the body's
375	top level. Forbidden constructs include `if`, `try`/`catch`, `foreach`,
376	`while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||` operators, `;`-separated statements,
377	intermediate `$var` assignments, and ternary `?:`. A single `if`-guarded
378	effect like `if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden
379	— even though it has only one "real" effect, the body contains a decision
380	(the test) and an action (the consequent). Decisions belong in bash. See
381	"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below for the canonical procedure.
382	
383	If a body needs N intermediate values or N decisions, run N round-trips:
384	capture state via `zbujb_powershell_capture` into bash variables, decide
385	in bash, dispatch unconditional effects via `zbujb_admin_powershell`. Bash
386	owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect.
387	
388	Empirical baseline: every `zbujb_admin_powershell` call site in the module
389	follows single-expression shape — `Get-LocalUser ...`, `Test-Path ...`,
390	`Get-Acl ...`, `icacls ...`, `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`, etc. The original
391	violator (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`, profile registration via
392	`$sid=...; $path=...; New-Item; New-ItemProperty`) produced a quoting
393	failure (reg.exe `Invalid syntax` on the `Windows NT` space) because the
394	compound shape interleaved PS interpolation, native-binary argv handling,
395	and registry-path building in one body. Decomposing into bash-orchestrated
396	single-expression calls eliminated the failure class. A second documented
397	violation cycle in
398	`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` records
399	the `if`-as-guard rationalization that motivates the explicit
400	forbidden-constructs list above.
401	
402	Exception: the wrapper-side prelude
403	(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; ${z_body}; if ...`)
404	is library code written once and exercised by all callers. Caller-side
405	bodies follow the single-expression rule.
406	
407	```bash
408	# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
409	zbujb_admin_powershell "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
410	
411	# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
412	zbujb_admin_powershell "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
413	
414	# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
415	z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") || buc_die "..."
416	z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
417	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
418	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
419	
420	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
421	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
422	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
423	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
424	fi
425	```
426	
427	### ❌ PS-6: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them
428	
429	If a remote command needs a string with embedded variables (registry path,
430	file path, SID), the bash caller builds the string by expanding
431	`${bash_var}` into the body before sending. The PS body receives the
432	already-resolved string as a literal (PS single-quoted, or
433	argument-bound).
434	
435	Rationale: bash escape rules cascade once. The bash → ssh → cmd.exe →
436	PS-literal-string chain is well-understood. Adding PS-side interpolation
437	(`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution model on top; subtle bugs
438	(like the PS-7 native-binary space-quoting trap) compound across layers
439	and become harder to diagnose.
440	
441	```bash
442	# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
443	zbujb_admin_powershell "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"
444	
445	# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
446	z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
447	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
448	```
449	
450	### ❌ PS-7: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries
451	
452	PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
453	schtasks.exe) has documented quirks with embedded spaces and quotes.
454	A registry path containing `Windows NT` (one space) breaks `reg.exe`
455	invoked through PS even when the path is held in a PS variable — empirical
456	in this session's diagnostic cycle (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`,
457	pre-decomposition).
458	
459	Rule: for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive via `New-Item` /
460	`New-ItemProperty`, ACLs via `Get-Acl`, account ops via `Get-LocalUser`),
461	use PS cmdlets — they handle their own paths transparently. For
462	native-binary effects (reg.exe, schtasks.exe), either pass already-resolved
463	bash literals that don't traverse PS interpolation, or drop the PS layer
464	entirely and invoke through cmd.exe directly (OpenSSH-Win32's default
465	shell).
466	
467	```bash
468	# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
469	zbujb_admin_powershell "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"
470	
471	# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
472	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
473	```
474	
475	### ❌ PS-8: Error suppression is not idempotency
476	
477	When a destructive cmdlet (`Remove-Item`, `Unregister-*`, `Stop-Service`,
478	`Remove-LocalUser`) might fail because its target is absent, the temptation
479	is to reach for an error-suppression flag and call the body "idempotent":
480	
481	- `-ErrorAction Ignore`
482	- `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`
483	- `2>$null` redirection
484	- `try { ... } catch { }` with empty or discarding catch
485	
486	Forbidden on destructive actions. Error-suppression flags swallow **every**
487	error class, not just "target absent." Permission denied, file locked, ACL
488	refusal, path-too-long, provider-not-loaded — all silently absorbed. The
489	cmdlet returns success, `$LASTEXITCODE` stays 0, the wrapper's trailer
490	takes the no-fire branch, and bash sees a clean exit. The destructive
491	action might not have actually happened, and we have no way to know.
492	
493	This is the "silently swallowed" failure class WSG opens with (Core
494	Philosophy, *Trap, don't trust*). Idempotency MUST come from explicit
495	state capture in bash followed by a conditional unconditional action — see
496	"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below.
497	
498	Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**. `New-Item
499	... | Out-Null` discards the cmdlet's *return object* and is a routine
500	hygiene practice (the cmdlet still throws on actual error). `Remove-Item
501	... -ErrorAction Ignore` suppresses *errors*, converting "permission
502	denied" into apparent success. PS-8 forbids the latter on destructive
503	actions.
504	
505	Probe-side use is permitted. `Get-LocalUser -Name '...' -ErrorAction
506	SilentlyContinue` on a non-destructive query, where "user not found" is
507	the answer we want and stdout-empty is how we read it, is exactly correct.
508	The carve-out: error suppression is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is
509	to *return state*, forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.
510	
511	```bash
512	# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
513	zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
514	zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
515	zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"
516	
517	# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
518	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
519	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
520	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
521	fi
522	
523	# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
524	zbujb_admin_powershell "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
525	```
526	
527	Empirical site: this rule was minted after a session in which `-ErrorAction
528	Ignore` was reached for to escape a PS-5 `if`-guard violation, substituting
529	an OQ-1 silent-swallow for the structural one. See
530	`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md`.
531	
532	## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern
533	
534	PS-5's closing line — *"Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body
535	is one effect"* — names the canonical procedure for any decision that
536	would otherwise live in a PS body. Capture-Decide-Dispatch (CDD) is a
537	three-step recipe:
538	
539	1. **Capture** — `zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE "<single-expression probe>"`
540	   runs a PS-5-clean probe (cmdlet call or native binary call, no `if`,
541	   no compounds) and returns its stdout into a bash variable. The wrapper
542	   strips Windows CR (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:487`); the bash variable holds
543	   clean text. Absorb capture failure with `|| buc_die "..."` if the probe
544	   must succeed, or `|| z_var=""` if a fundus-side absence is tolerable
545	   (e.g. probing for state on a host that may not yet have the relevant
546	   subsystem installed).
547	
548	2. **Decide** — bash conditional. For PS booleans, see "PowerShell boolean
549	   serialization" below. For text/list output, use
550	   `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$z_var"` for exact-line membership, or
551	   `case ... in ... esac` / `[[ "$z_var" == ... ]]` for value compare.
552	
553	3. **Dispatch** — `zbujb_admin_powershell "<single-expression effect>"`
554	   runs the unconditional action only on the bash-side branches that need
555	   it. Each branch uses one zbujb call.
556	
557	Concrete shapes for common cases — file/dir presence, WSL distro
558	membership, local user existence, service state, registry key existence —
559	appear in the "Idempotency Exemplars" section below.
560	
561	### PowerShell boolean serialization
562	
563	`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets serialize
564	as the literal strings `"True"` or `"False"` through
565	`zbujb_powershell_capture` (after WSL_UTF8 normalization and CR stripping).
566	Canonical bash check:
567	
568	```bash
569	if [[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]; then ...
570	```
571	
572	Do not use `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is also non-empty.
573	
574	### Capture failure absorption
575	
576	The capture call's bash exit-status is non-zero on any of: ssh transport
577	failure, PS body trapping its own error under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`,
578	native binary in the body returning non-zero, or wrapper-trailer fire on
579	`$LASTEXITCODE`. Two absorption shapes:
580	
581	```bash
582	# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
583	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
584	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
585	
586	# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
587	z_wsl_list=""
588	z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
589	  || z_wsl_list=""
590	```
591	
592	Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
593	state is a legitimate pre-condition (e.g. WSL not yet installed during
594	first-run purge — the next steps install it).
595	
596	### Open question: bool-returning predicate variant
597	
598	A `zbujb_powershell_predicate` (BCG `_predicate` class — never dies, status
599	only, idiomatic in `if`/`while`) would collapse the capture+compare pair
600	into `if zbujb_powershell_predicate ROLE "Test-Path '...'"; then ...`. The
601	shape was tabled because ssh-layer transport failure adds a third value
602	that doesn't fit either bool answer cleanly: strict BCG predicate semantics
603	("never dies, status only") would conflate transport failure with "false,"
604	reintroducing the OQ-1 silent-swallow this section is meant to close. The
605	canonical CDD shape uses `zbujb_powershell_capture` + `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
606	explicitly so transport failures stay loud. The predicate variant remains
607	an open design question.
608	
609	## Idempotency Exemplars
610	
611	Ready-made CDD shapes for the patterns this codebase actually uses. Copy
612	and adjust the variable names; do not derive from principles each time.
613	Adding a new exemplar earns a paragraph here once verified.
614	
615	### File presence → conditional remove
616	
617	```bash
618	local z_present
619	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
620	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
621	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
622	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
623	    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
624	fi
625	```
626	
627	### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)
628	
629	```bash
630	local z_present
631	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
632	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
633	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
634	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
635	    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
636	fi
637	```
638	
639	### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister
640	
641	A fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
642	the list capture absorbs failure silently rather than dying.
643	
644	```bash
645	local z_wsl_list=""
646	z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
647	  || z_wsl_list=""
648	if grep -qFx "${z_dist}" <<<"${z_wsl_list}"; then
649	  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
650	    || buc_die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"
651	fi
652	```
653	
654	### Local user existence → conditional removal
655	
656	`Get-LocalUser`'s `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` is permitted here per
657	PS-8's probe-side carve-out — the cmdlet is non-destructive and "user
658	absent" reads as empty stdout.
659	
660	```bash
661	local z_user_state
662	z_user_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
663	  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
664	  || buc_die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
665	z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
666	if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
667	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
668	    || buc_die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
669	fi
670	```
671	
672	### Service running state → conditional start
673	
674	```bash
675	local z_service_state
676	z_service_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
677	  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
678	  || buc_die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
679	if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
680	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
681	    || buc_die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
682	fi
683	```
684	
685	### Registry key absence → conditional creation
686	
687	```bash
688	local z_present
689	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
690	  || buc_die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
691	if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
692	  zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
693	    || buc_die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"
694	fi
695	```
696	
697	## Wrapper Discipline
698	
699	### PowerShell wrapper (proven shape)
700	
701	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_powershell` after the LASTEXITCODE-init repair:
702	
703	```bash
704	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
705	    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
706	```
707	
708	Discipline:
709	- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
710	- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
711	- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (PS-1)
712	- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
713	- Body uses single quotes for PS literals (PS-3)
714	- Body avoids object-emitting cmdlets immediately before exit, or pipes
715	  them through Out-String first (PS-2)
716	
717	### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (proven shape, w-letter)
718	
719	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_exec w` after the cycle-3 BCG-clean repair. Body is
720	`;`-joined for one-line transit (SH-2), `"` escaped to `\"` (SH-3), and
721	all `$name`/`${name}` references escaped to `\$name`/`\${name}` for
722	deferred bash-side expansion (SH-6). `$(...)` command substitution does
723	not need escape (SH-6).
724	
725	```bash
726	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
727	    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body}\""
728	```
729	
730	Body authoring discipline:
731	- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
732	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
733	- `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}` (SH-6)
734	- `$(...)` left as-is (SH-6)
735	- No heredocs, no `bash -s`, no inner pipe-then-bash (SH-1, SH-5)
736	- No newlines in body (SH-2); use SH-8 if the body genuinely won't fit
737	
738	### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (proven shape, c-letter)
739	
740	Cygwin's path is simpler. cmd.exe → C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe. No wsl.exe,
741	no Windows-side `$` substitution.
742	
743	```bash
744	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
745	    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body}\""
746	```
747	
748	Body authoring discipline:
749	- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
750	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
751	- `$name`, `${name}`, `$(...)` all unescaped — bash sees them directly (SH-6)
752	- No heredocs, no `bash -s` (SH-1, SH-5)
753	
754	## Deferred — out of release-1 scope
755	
756	### b-letter (Linux/Mac native ssh) verification
757	
758	No Linux or Mac BURN profile is registered in the release-1 matrix; only
759	`bujn-winpc` (`BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows`). The b-letter path bypasses
760	every Windows-specific layer (no cmd.exe DefaultShell, no wsl.exe argv
761	substitution, no Windows argv parser); standard BCG body discipline
762	applies. If a Linux/Mac BURN profile is added, run the OQ-1 probes from
763	`Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` (probes 1A, 1G,
764	1J, 1P) substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin to confirm absence
765	of Windows-layer quirks. See §OQ-3 of that memo.
766	
767	## Empirical Record
768	
769	Probe matrices, raw transcripts, and the bisection narratives that
770	generated the rules in this document are filed under `Memos/`. WSG cites
771	those memos but does not duplicate their contents.
772	
773	Active references:
774	
775	- `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` — initial OQ-1
776	  through OQ-7 resolution matrix run against `bujn-winpc` (pace `₢A-AA0`).
777	- `Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` —
778	  PS-5 `if`-as-guard rationalization and `-ErrorAction Ignore` substitution
779	  cycles; motivates the "Rules enumerate" Core Philosophy clause, the PS-5
780	  exclusive-enumeration tightening, the new PS-8 rule, and the
781	  Capture-Decide-Dispatch named pattern.
782	
783	### Convention for future Windows-transport experiments
784	
785	When a new open question surfaces around the Windows transport stack
786	(wsl.exe, cygwin, cmd.exe, OpenSSH-Win32, PowerShell), file the probe
787	matrix and resolution at:
788	
789	```
790	Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md
791	```
792	
793	Cite the memo from WSG once findings stabilize into rules. WSG retains
794	the distilled rules and one-line probe pairs; memos retain the full
795	empirical record.
796	
797	## Verification Probe Template
798	
799	Every WSG rule has a probe that demonstrates the failure mode and the
800	fix. Standard form:
801	
802	```
803	# Probe N: <claim>
804	# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>
805	
806	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<failing form>"
807	# stdout: <observed>
808	# exit:   <observed>
809	
810	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<repaired form>"
811	# stdout: <observed>
812	# exit:   <observed>
813	```
814	
815	Probes are read-only and idempotent where possible. Probes that mutate
816	remote state (creating users, files) must declare cleanup or rely on a
817	subsequent obliterate cycle.
818	
819	## Acronym Registry
820	
821	| Term | Expansion |
822	|------|-----------|
823	| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |
824	| OpenSSH-Win32 | The Win32 port of OpenSSH bundled with Windows 10+ |
825	| Curia | The local invoking machine (per JJK terminology) |
826	| Fundus | The remote target machine (per JJK terminology) |
827	| LASTEXITCODE-null trap | The `$null -ne 0 → True` issue in PS-1 |
828	| Stdin-consumption | The FD-0-inherited-by-child issue in SH-1 |
829	| Body-as-arg | The `bash -c "<body>"` shape that avoids SH-1 |
830	
831	## Related Documents
832	
833	- BCG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) — base bash discipline
834	- BUS0 (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`) — BUK utilities
835	  spec; covers temp file forensic preservation under `BURD_TEMP_DIR`
836	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` — current implementation seat for
837	  fenestrate/garrison wrappers (`zbujb_admin_powershell`, `zbujb_admin_exec`)
838

file.content:
# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide

## Purpose

WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.

WSG is empirically derived. Every established rule has a verification probe
that demonstrates the failure mode the rule prevents. Open questions are
flagged explicitly and resolved by separate experiment paces, not by
conjecture.

## Core Philosophy

**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in curia
bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser, another
in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The transport
**transforms** the body at each boundary. Errors at any layer can be
silently swallowed if not explicitly trapped.

**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** The user's command is wrapped
in `cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
rules — `"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling —
bite even bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.

**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
output (Write-Host, Write-Output of strings) eagerly; some cmdlets'
formatter cycles flush only on full pipeline completion, so calling
`exit` mid-body (or trailer-side) discards their unformatted output.
The set of lazy cmdlets is not universal — `Get-LocalUser` is proven
lazy, `Get-Item` is proven eager. Treat the lazy default as the safe
assumption (PS-2). String emission always survives.

**Trap, don't trust.** Each rule below assumes that any error which can
plausibly be silenced WILL be silenced if not explicitly trapped. The bias
is toward defensive instrumentation: surface every non-zero exit, every
empty stdout, every unflushed buffer, with a forensic temp file naming the
boundary that swallowed it.

**Rules enumerate; they don't illustrate.** Each rule below names a closed
set of allowed shapes. Anything not enumerated is forbidden, even if it
*looks* morally similar to an allowed form. Do not reason "this is *like*
one expression because it's *like* one effect" — that path admits a fourth
shape the rule never sanctioned, and the rationalization propagates to
every call site that copies the precedent. When in doubt, decompose into
more round-trips: the cost of an extra ssh hop is small; the cost of a
loophole is structural.

## Transport Stack

A typical chain for `zbujb_admin_powershell` or `zbujb_admin_exec`:

```
1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
5. Child shell (one of):
   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
```

Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.

## Established Rules

### ❌ PS-1: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init

In a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run,
`$LASTEXITCODE` is `$null`. PowerShell's typed comparison treats `$null -ne 0`
as `True`. A trailer like `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }`
always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (which exits 0 in
practice). The `exit` short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter
pipeline, discarding any cmdlet output buffered by Out-Default that hasn't
flushed to stdout yet.

```powershell
# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"

# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
```

Probe pair (verification):

```
./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
# stdout → CRLF only

./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$LASTEXITCODE = 0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
# stdout → full LocalUser table
```

### ❌ PS-2: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush

PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but *some* cmdlet object outputs
lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render). `exit` aborts the formatter
pipeline. Lazy outputs that haven't flushed are discarded. The cmdlets
that exhibit this lazy behavior are formatter-cycle-specific: `Get-LocalUser`
from `Microsoft.PowerShell.LocalAccounts` is proven lazy (empty stdout
when followed by `exit 0`); `Get-Item` from the FileSystem provider is
proven eager (table survives `exit 0`). Treat the rule as the safe
default: assume any cmdlet output is lazy unless you've checked. See
the OQ-6 section of `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
for the empirical baseline.

```powershell
# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"

# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
```

If a wrapper provides `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` initialization (PS-1), this rule
is moot for happy-path bodies. Failure paths still need string-materialization
guards if any cmdlet output must survive the trailer's exit branch.

### ❌ SH-1: Script via stdin to bash (heredoc form / `bash -s`)

When ssh feeds a script to remote bash via stdin — `bash -s` on the remote
with heredoc on the local side, or `echo BODY | bash` chains on the remote —
bash reads commands from FD 0. Child processes spawned by that bash inherit
FD 0, connected to the script-providing pipe. A child that reads FD 0
(notably Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop) consumes script bytes
bash hadn't yet parsed. Bash hits EOF prematurely, exits 0 without errors,
and remaining commands silently never execute.

This is BCG's stdin-consumption discipline (BCG line 1388) at a different
scope.

```bash
# ❌ Script via heredoc/stdin; net.exe (or any FD-0 reader) eats bytes
ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -s" <<SCRIPT
set -euo pipefail
net.exe user 'foo' /add ... > /dev/null
useradd 'foo'                       # may silently never run
SCRIPT

# ✅ Body as bash -c argument; bash reads body from -c, not FD 0
ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -euo pipefail; net.exe ...; useradd 'foo'\""
```

Verification: cycle-1 vs cycle-3 of correct-wsl-user-model (`₢A-AAv`).
Heredoc form silently dropped useradd; args form propagated correctly,
proven by `getent passwd bujuw_user` returning the entry post-step-3.

### ❌ SH-2: Multi-line bodies through cmd.exe

Cmd.exe is line-oriented. Newlines in `"..."` quoted args are unreliable
across the cmd.exe → child-binary boundary. Use `;` as the bash command
separator and ship the body as one line.

```bash
# ❌ Newlines in body; cmd.exe may fragment
ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"
set -e
useradd foo
\""

# ✅ Single-line body with ; separators
ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"set -e;useradd foo\""
```

### ✅ SH-3: Outer `"..."` with `\"` for embedded double-quotes

The Windows argv parser (used by wsl.exe and most Windows binaries) honors
`\"` inside `"..."` as an escape for literal `"`. This survives cmd.exe and
reaches Linux bash as `"`. Cmd.exe itself does NOT process `\"`; it passes
the bytes to the child whose argv parser handles the escape.

```bash
# ✅ Embedded " in body
ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"trap 'rm -f \\\"\$path\\\"' EXIT\""
```

Probe: `bash -c "echo SAW: \"hello\""` → stdout `SAW: hello` (quotes
stripped by bash's own parsing of the unescaped `"`).

### ✅ PS-3: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting

Inside the outer `"..."` of `powershell -Command "..."`, single-quoted
strings (`'Stop'`, `'username'`) pass through cleanly. Cmd.exe does not
honor single quotes for arg-grouping but treats them as literal characters;
PowerShell parses its body and recognizes `'...'` as PS string literals.

```bash
# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user'\""
```

This is why `zbujb_admin_powershell` consistently uses single quotes for PS
string literals — they nest reliably through every layer.

### ✅ PS-4: PS-2's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic

The PS-2 lazy-flush is a property of the PowerShell body, not of how
PowerShell was launched. The same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits
empty stdout via direct cmd.exe→powershell, cygwin→powershell.exe, and
wsl.exe→bash→powershell.exe. The fix (Out-String materialization, or
PS-1's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer doesn't fire) is also
transport-agnostic. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
§OQ-6.

### ❌ SH-4: cmd.exe does NOT process `$()` or `$var`

Verified by direct probe: `cmd.exe /c echo "SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` emits the
literal string `"SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` with no expansion. Cmd.exe's
variable syntax is `%var%` and it has no command substitution. Therefore
the outer cmd.exe layer is NOT the layer that processes shell variable
expansion or command substitution in transit.

This rule is structural; the corollary is OQ-1 (something else does eat `$`
in some chains).

### ❌ SH-5: BCG bans extend across the transport boundary

The bash code we author at the curia AND the bash code we author for remote
execution are both subject to BCG. In particular:

- **No heredocs anywhere.** The transport must not use `bash -s` with
  heredoc input (SH-1 covers the failure mode); module callers must not
  build remote bodies via heredoc.
- **No pipelines inside `$()`.** This applies to module code (e.g. the
  ill-fated `z_b64=$(printf ... | base64 | tr -d ...)` transport) and to
  bodies authored for remote execution.
- **No unguarded `$()`.** Remote bodies that use command substitution must
  guard against silent failure the same way curia code does.

The `mktemp` exception in BCG line 1473 (introspection allowlist) carries
through; remote bodies may use `$(mktemp)` for temp file creation. Top-level
pipelines (not inside `$()`) are not banned by BCG and are permitted in
remote bodies (see e.g. step-5's `openssl enc -base64 -d`).

### ❌ SH-6: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv

The wsl.exe argv-to-Linux-invocation processor performs `$name` and
`${name}` substitution against its own (Linux-side, root-shell startup)
environment before bash receives the body. Names present in that
environment substitute to their values; undefined names substitute to
empty. **Cygwin bash does not exhibit this behavior. cmd.exe alone (per
SH-4) also does not.** `$(...)` command substitution is unaffected (the
`(` after `$` does not match the `$name`/`${name}` token shape).

Per-letter escape table:

| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |

Probe pair (verification, w-letter):

```
# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
# stdout: (empty)

# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
# stdout: HELLO
```

See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-1, §OQ-2,
§OQ-4.

### ✅ SH-7: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports

Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through every tested transport:
cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe → bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and native
Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop. `set -e` aborts the script
on first failure and the failing exit reaches the curia unchanged.
Bodies that need to fail-loud should use `set -e` or explicit `exit N`.

Probe (w-letter, set -e + false):

```
./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "set -e; false; echo UNREACHED"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
# (no UNREACHED in stdout)
# EXIT=1
```

See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-5.

### ✅ SH-8: Multi-line bodies via file feed (when SH-2 doesn't fit)

When a body materially exceeds what `;`-join (SH-2) can express, the
canonical pattern is two-phase: phase 1 ships the body as a remote file
via PowerShell `Set-Content` or `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText`; phase 2
runs `wsl.exe ... bash /path/to/file` (or `cygwin bash --login -c "bash
/path/to/file"`). Side benefit: bypasses wsl.exe's argv `$`-substitution
(SH-6) since the script body comes from disk, not argv.

Caveats:
- PS `Set-Content` writes CRLF. Either use an LF-explicit writer:

  ```powershell
  [System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, ($lines -join "`n"))
  ```

  or normalize CRLF→LF into a *second* file before exec.
- Do NOT pipe the file into `bash` via stdin; that reintroduces SH-1's
  stdin-consumption hazard. Use `bash /path/to/file`, not `bash <file`.
- The two-phase approach is not atomic; the caller flow must clean up
  the remote temp file.

Default discipline remains SH-2; SH-8 applies only when bodies don't fit
one line. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
§OQ-7.

### ❌ SH-9: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport

When a command line is routed `bash (curia) → ssh → Windows OpenSSH →
cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with no PowerShell or remote bash
layer to interpret quotes, single quotes around args are passed
through to the binary as part of its argv. This is the negative form
of PS-3.

```bash
# ❌ Single quotes survive cmd.exe and become part of wsl.exe's argv.
# wsl.exe sees the path as `'C:\path'` (literal quotes) and fails.
ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs' '...' --version 2"

# ✅ Double quotes via `\"...\"`. After bash escape they reach the wire
# as `"..."`; cmd.exe strips them per its native argv parser; wsl.exe
# receives clean paths.
ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
```

The asymmetry: PS-3 (single quotes survive nesting) applies when there
is a PowerShell layer that recognizes `'...'` as a string-literal
delimiter. SH-9 applies when there is no such layer — only cmd.exe
between the wire and the binary. Single quotes are not a
transport-universal "safe" choice; they require a layer that
interprets them.

Decision rule: pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter** in
the transport stack:

| Innermost interpreter | Quote form for embedded args |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| PowerShell            | `'...'` (PS string literal)   |
| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)  |
| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips) |

Empirical site: `zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl` in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
— the `wsl --import` invocation in garrison-w's three-namespace
redesign (BUSJGW).

### ❌ PS-5: PowerShell bodies are single expressions

A `zbujb_admin_powershell` (or `zbujb_powershell_capture`) body MUST be
exactly one of these three shapes — no others:

1. One cmdlet call (with arguments, parameters, and pipelined formatters
   like `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
2. One native binary call (with arguments).
3. One native binary with an inner-shell body (e.g.
   `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).

The enumeration is constructive (Core Philosophy, *Rules enumerate; they
don't illustrate*). No other PowerShell construct may appear at the body's
top level. Forbidden constructs include `if`, `try`/`catch`, `foreach`,
`while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||` operators, `;`-separated statements,
intermediate `$var` assignments, and ternary `?:`. A single `if`-guarded
effect like `if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden
— even though it has only one "real" effect, the body contains a decision
(the test) and an action (the consequent). Decisions belong in bash. See
"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below for the canonical procedure.

If a body needs N intermediate values or N decisions, run N round-trips:
capture state via `zbujb_powershell_capture` into bash variables, decide
in bash, dispatch unconditional effects via `zbujb_admin_powershell`. Bash
owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect.

Empirical baseline: every `zbujb_admin_powershell` call site in the module
follows single-expression shape — `Get-LocalUser ...`, `Test-Path ...`,
`Get-Acl ...`, `icacls ...`, `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`, etc. The original
violator (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`, profile registration via
`$sid=...; $path=...; New-Item; New-ItemProperty`) produced a quoting
failure (reg.exe `Invalid syntax` on the `Windows NT` space) because the
compound shape interleaved PS interpolation, native-binary argv handling,
and registry-path building in one body. Decomposing into bash-orchestrated
single-expression calls eliminated the failure class. A second documented
violation cycle in
`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` records
the `if`-as-guard rationalization that motivates the explicit
forbidden-constructs list above.

Exception: the wrapper-side prelude
(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; ${z_body}; if ...`)
is library code written once and exercised by all callers. Caller-side
bodies follow the single-expression rule.

```bash
# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
zbujb_admin_powershell "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"

# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
zbujb_admin_powershell "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"

# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") || buc_die "..."
z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"

# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
fi
```

### ❌ PS-6: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them

If a remote command needs a string with embedded variables (registry path,
file path, SID), the bash caller builds the string by expanding
`${bash_var}` into the body before sending. The PS body receives the
already-resolved string as a literal (PS single-quoted, or
argument-bound).

Rationale: bash escape rules cascade once. The bash → ssh → cmd.exe →
PS-literal-string chain is well-understood. Adding PS-side interpolation
(`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution model on top; subtle bugs
(like the PS-7 native-binary space-quoting trap) compound across layers
and become harder to diagnose.

```bash
# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
zbujb_admin_powershell "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"

# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
```

### ❌ PS-7: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries

PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
schtasks.exe) has documented quirks with embedded spaces and quotes.
A registry path containing `Windows NT` (one space) breaks `reg.exe`
invoked through PS even when the path is held in a PS variable — empirical
in this session's diagnostic cycle (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`,
pre-decomposition).

Rule: for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive via `New-Item` /
`New-ItemProperty`, ACLs via `Get-Acl`, account ops via `Get-LocalUser`),
use PS cmdlets — they handle their own paths transparently. For
native-binary effects (reg.exe, schtasks.exe), either pass already-resolved
bash literals that don't traverse PS interpolation, or drop the PS layer
entirely and invoke through cmd.exe directly (OpenSSH-Win32's default
shell).

```bash
# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
zbujb_admin_powershell "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"

# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
```

### ❌ PS-8: Error suppression is not idempotency

When a destructive cmdlet (`Remove-Item`, `Unregister-*`, `Stop-Service`,
`Remove-LocalUser`) might fail because its target is absent, the temptation
is to reach for an error-suppression flag and call the body "idempotent":

- `-ErrorAction Ignore`
- `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`
- `2>$null` redirection
- `try { ... } catch { }` with empty or discarding catch

Forbidden on destructive actions. Error-suppression flags swallow **every**
error class, not just "target absent." Permission denied, file locked, ACL
refusal, path-too-long, provider-not-loaded — all silently absorbed. The
cmdlet returns success, `$LASTEXITCODE` stays 0, the wrapper's trailer
takes the no-fire branch, and bash sees a clean exit. The destructive
action might not have actually happened, and we have no way to know.

This is the "silently swallowed" failure class WSG opens with (Core
Philosophy, *Trap, don't trust*). Idempotency MUST come from explicit
state capture in bash followed by a conditional unconditional action — see
"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below.

Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**. `New-Item
... | Out-Null` discards the cmdlet's *return object* and is a routine
hygiene practice (the cmdlet still throws on actual error). `Remove-Item
... -ErrorAction Ignore` suppresses *errors*, converting "permission
denied" into apparent success. PS-8 forbids the latter on destructive
actions.

Probe-side use is permitted. `Get-LocalUser -Name '...' -ErrorAction
SilentlyContinue` on a non-destructive query, where "user not found" is
the answer we want and stdout-empty is how we read it, is exactly correct.
The carve-out: error suppression is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is
to *return state*, forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.

```bash
# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"

# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
fi

# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
zbujb_admin_powershell "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
```

Empirical site: this rule was minted after a session in which `-ErrorAction
Ignore` was reached for to escape a PS-5 `if`-guard violation, substituting
an OQ-1 silent-swallow for the structural one. See
`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md`.

## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern

PS-5's closing line — *"Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body
is one effect"* — names the canonical procedure for any decision that
would otherwise live in a PS body. Capture-Decide-Dispatch (CDD) is a
three-step recipe:

1. **Capture** — `zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE "<single-expression probe>"`
   runs a PS-5-clean probe (cmdlet call or native binary call, no `if`,
   no compounds) and returns its stdout into a bash variable. The wrapper
   strips Windows CR (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:487`); the bash variable holds
   clean text. Absorb capture failure with `|| buc_die "..."` if the probe
   must succeed, or `|| z_var=""` if a fundus-side absence is tolerable
   (e.g. probing for state on a host that may not yet have the relevant
   subsystem installed).

2. **Decide** — bash conditional. For PS booleans, see "PowerShell boolean
   serialization" below. For text/list output, use
   `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$z_var"` for exact-line membership, or
   `case ... in ... esac` / `[[ "$z_var" == ... ]]` for value compare.

3. **Dispatch** — `zbujb_admin_powershell "<single-expression effect>"`
   runs the unconditional action only on the bash-side branches that need
   it. Each branch uses one zbujb call.

Concrete shapes for common cases — file/dir presence, WSL distro
membership, local user existence, service state, registry key existence —
appear in the "Idempotency Exemplars" section below.

### PowerShell boolean serialization

`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets serialize
as the literal strings `"True"` or `"False"` through
`zbujb_powershell_capture` (after WSL_UTF8 normalization and CR stripping).
Canonical bash check:

```bash
if [[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]; then ...
```

Do not use `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is also non-empty.

### Capture failure absorption

The capture call's bash exit-status is non-zero on any of: ssh transport
failure, PS body trapping its own error under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`,
native binary in the body returning non-zero, or wrapper-trailer fire on
`$LASTEXITCODE`. Two absorption shapes:

```bash
# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"

# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
z_wsl_list=""
z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
  || z_wsl_list=""
```

Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
state is a legitimate pre-condition (e.g. WSL not yet installed during
first-run purge — the next steps install it).

### Open question: bool-returning predicate variant

A `zbujb_powershell_predicate` (BCG `_predicate` class — never dies, status
only, idiomatic in `if`/`while`) would collapse the capture+compare pair
into `if zbujb_powershell_predicate ROLE "Test-Path '...'"; then ...`. The
shape was tabled because ssh-layer transport failure adds a third value
that doesn't fit either bool answer cleanly: strict BCG predicate semantics
("never dies, status only") would conflate transport failure with "false,"
reintroducing the OQ-1 silent-swallow this section is meant to close. The
canonical CDD shape uses `zbujb_powershell_capture` + `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
explicitly so transport failures stay loud. The predicate variant remains
an open design question.

## Idempotency Exemplars

Ready-made CDD shapes for the patterns this codebase actually uses. Copy
and adjust the variable names; do not derive from principles each time.
Adding a new exemplar earns a paragraph here once verified.

### File presence → conditional remove

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
fi
```

### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
fi
```

### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister

A fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
the list capture absorbs failure silently rather than dying.

```bash
local z_wsl_list=""
z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
  || z_wsl_list=""
if grep -qFx "${z_dist}" <<<"${z_wsl_list}"; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"
fi
```

### Local user existence → conditional removal

`Get-LocalUser`'s `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` is permitted here per
PS-8's probe-side carve-out — the cmdlet is non-destructive and "user
absent" reads as empty stdout.

```bash
local z_user_state
z_user_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
fi
```

### Service running state → conditional start

```bash
local z_service_state
z_service_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
    || buc_die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
fi
```

### Registry key absence → conditional creation

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
  || buc_die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
  zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
    || buc_die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"
fi
```

## Wrapper Discipline

### PowerShell wrapper (proven shape)

Mirrors `zbujb_admin_powershell` after the LASTEXITCODE-init repair:

```bash
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
```

Discipline:
- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (PS-1)
- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
- Body uses single quotes for PS literals (PS-3)
- Body avoids object-emitting cmdlets immediately before exit, or pipes
  them through Out-String first (PS-2)

### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (proven shape, w-letter)

Mirrors `zbujb_admin_exec w` after the cycle-3 BCG-clean repair. Body is
`;`-joined for one-line transit (SH-2), `"` escaped to `\"` (SH-3), and
all `$name`/`${name}` references escaped to `\$name`/`\${name}` for
deferred bash-side expansion (SH-6). `$(...)` command substitution does
not need escape (SH-6).

```bash
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body}\""
```

Body authoring discipline:
- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
- `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}` (SH-6)
- `$(...)` left as-is (SH-6)
- No heredocs, no `bash -s`, no inner pipe-then-bash (SH-1, SH-5)
- No newlines in body (SH-2); use SH-8 if the body genuinely won't fit

### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (proven shape, c-letter)

Cygwin's path is simpler. cmd.exe → C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe. No wsl.exe,
no Windows-side `$` substitution.

```bash
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body}\""
```

Body authoring discipline:
- Statements joined with `;` (SH-2)
- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
- `$name`, `${name}`, `$(...)` all unescaped — bash sees them directly (SH-6)
- No heredocs, no `bash -s` (SH-1, SH-5)

## Deferred — out of release-1 scope

### b-letter (Linux/Mac native ssh) verification

No Linux or Mac BURN profile is registered in the release-1 matrix; only
`bujn-winpc` (`BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows`). The b-letter path bypasses
every Windows-specific layer (no cmd.exe DefaultShell, no wsl.exe argv
substitution, no Windows argv parser); standard BCG body discipline
applies. If a Linux/Mac BURN profile is added, run the OQ-1 probes from
`Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` (probes 1A, 1G,
1J, 1P) substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin to confirm absence
of Windows-layer quirks. See §OQ-3 of that memo.

## Empirical Record

Probe matrices, raw transcripts, and the bisection narratives that
generated the rules in this document are filed under `Memos/`. WSG cites
those memos but does not duplicate their contents.

Active references:

- `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` — initial OQ-1
  through OQ-7 resolution matrix run against `bujn-winpc` (pace `₢A-AA0`).
- `Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` —
  PS-5 `if`-as-guard rationalization and `-ErrorAction Ignore` substitution
  cycles; motivates the "Rules enumerate" Core Philosophy clause, the PS-5
  exclusive-enumeration tightening, the new PS-8 rule, and the
  Capture-Decide-Dispatch named pattern.

### Convention for future Windows-transport experiments

When a new open question surfaces around the Windows transport stack
(wsl.exe, cygwin, cmd.exe, OpenSSH-Win32, PowerShell), file the probe
matrix and resolution at:

```
Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-windows-transport-{topic}.md
```

Cite the memo from WSG once findings stabilize into rules. WSG retains
the distilled rules and one-line probe pairs; memos retain the full
empirical record.

## Verification Probe Template

Every WSG rule has a probe that demonstrates the failure mode and the
fix. Standard form:

```
# Probe N: <claim>
# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>

./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<failing form>"
# stdout: <observed>
# exit:   <observed>

./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<repaired form>"
# stdout: <observed>
# exit:   <observed>
```

Probes are read-only and idempotent where possible. Probes that mutate
remote state (creating users, files) must declare cleanup or rely on a
subsequent obliterate cycle.

## Acronym Registry

| Term | Expansion |
|------|-----------|
| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |
| OpenSSH-Win32 | The Win32 port of OpenSSH bundled with Windows 10+ |
| Curia | The local invoking machine (per JJK terminology) |
| Fundus | The remote target machine (per JJK terminology) |
| LASTEXITCODE-null trap | The `$null -ne 0 → True` issue in PS-1 |
| Stdin-consumption | The FD-0-inherited-by-child issue in SH-1 |
| Body-as-arg | The `bash -c "<body>"` shape that avoids SH-1 |

400	# handles multi-line args poorly).
401	#
402	# This function does NOT transform $. Body authors are responsible for
403	# per-letter $-escape discipline (see WSG).
404	zbujb_admin_exec() {
405	  zbujb_sentinel
406	  local z_letter="${1:-}"
407	  zbujb_assert_shell_letter "${z_letter}"
408	  shift
409	  test $# -ge 1 \
410	    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_exec: at least one statement required"
411	
412	  # IFS-local join with ';' — pure parameter expansion, no subprocess.
413	  local IFS=';'
414	  local z_body="$*"
415	
416	  # Escape " → \" for the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer.
417	  local z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"
418	
419	  local z_remote_invoker
420	  case "${z_letter}" in
421	    b) z_remote_invoker='bash' ;;
422	    c) z_remote_invoker="${BUJB_path_cygwin_root_fwd}/bin/bash --login" ;;
423	    w) z_remote_invoker="wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root bash" ;;
424	  esac
425	
426	  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"        \
427	      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"             \
428	      -o BatchMode=yes                        \
429	      -o ConnectTimeout=15                    \
430	      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"  \
431	      "${z_remote_invoker} -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
432	}
433	
434	# zbujb_admin_powershell BODY... -- run a PowerShell statement chain on
435	# the remote node as the privileged user. Prepends discipline prelude:
436	# $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' (PS errors terminate), $env:WSL_UTF8=1
437	# (wsl.exe emits UTF-8), $LASTEXITCODE=0 (initialize so the trailing
438	# if-check doesn't trip on the $null default — $null -ne 0 evaluates True
439	# in PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
440	# discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like Get-LocalUser
441	# whose tables haven't flushed to stdout yet). Trailing if-check
442	# propagates $LASTEXITCODE on non-zero from native commands in the body.
443	# Caller redirects stdout/stderr; returns ssh's exit code.
444	zbujb_admin_powershell() {
445	  zbujb_sentinel
446	  test $# -ge 1 \
447	    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_powershell: PowerShell command body required"
448	  local -r z_body="$*"
449	
450	  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"        \
451	      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"             \
